So the issue with I have w/ this is that tenacity retry the generator itself instead of restarting it w/ the generator function.

Maybe that's too specific to how gRPC (ab)use of generator for streaming, but the semantic is the following:
```
stream = stub.StreamingMethodCall(req) # initiate the streaming gRPC call
for resp in stream:
    doSomething(resp)
```
The `stub.StreamingMethodCall()` is lazy and never raise exceptions, but the iteration itself can; and in case of a failure we want to retry it from the beginning (i.e: call `StreamingMethodCall`).

This makes it challenging for me to use `tenacity.Retrying` since it doesn't really expect its `callable` argument to be a generator function, nor it is capable of yielding result back to the caller.

This could work if we wrap the whole logic consuming the generator with `retry`.
```
def callable():
     for i in gen():
        doSomethingWith(i)
retry(callable)
```

But I was hoping I could insert `retry` between the generator itself and the code consuming it (so that for example an intermediate library could expose both lazy evaluation; provide retry logic and be decoupled from the code consuming it).

```
for i in retry(gen):
   doSomethingWith(i)
```

Let me know if that makes sense :)

`pew` will fork the `$SHELL` selected by the user, which means that it has to be able to resolve the executable name to its path

if `bash` is not inside `PATH` it won't be able to do that... if you have another shell (e.g. `fish`) inside `~/.local/bin`, as long as `SHELL` is set to `fish`, it'll work

You probably meant

    PATH=$PATH:~/.local/bin

instead of

    PATH=PATH:~/.local/bin

I believe this can be coded in a way using the ipaddress class and what it already offers and to quickly get a random ip address that is valid in any scenario. It's going to take a lot of refactoring, but should be worth it.

I'll try put together some kind of proof of concept when time permits and maybe make a determination from there.

It could be that I am missing something fundamental here.
A function F is well defined (deterministic) in it's behaviour:
`F(x1, x2, ..., xm; p1, p2, ..., po) -> Rn`
(How does math work in markdown? `F` maps variables `X` to an n-dimensional vector of real numbers)
But it may not be possible to express `F` using symbolic math easily (there might even be examples where this isn't possible at all), so it will not be possible to differentiate `F` symbolically.
In this frame of mind, it doesn't matter whether you have an ODE or something else; you define `F` which does the integration, normalization, and whatever more so that it maps `X` to `Rn`.
So in essence, I fail to see why ODE's are special snowflakes.

side-note: what do you call "well defined"?

Oh right, good catch about the destructor.  However, the right thing to do is to add an exception specification so that the error message is not hidden.  Otherwise, calling exit() is basically the same as what the compiler will do automatically which is call std::terminate().

So add "throw (std::exception)" to the end of the destructor declaration so the compiler knows this destructor is allowed to throw and I'll merge the pull request (and don't forget to remove the try/catch) :)
But this doesn't initialize the bias value.  You need to do something that changes the behavior of the setup() method.  That's where the initialization happens.

Hi Saloua, answering your questions:

**Resolving Actions**
In engine.py Ruleset init, you will notice how the 'run' object in each rule dictionary is stored away in the _actions variable.

```python
def __init__(self, name, host, ruleset_definition, state_cache_size):
    self._actions = {}
    for rule_name, rule in ruleset_definition.iteritems():
        action = rule['run']
        del rule['run']
        #other details removed for clarity
        elif (hasattr(action, '__call__')):
            self._actions[rule_name] = Promise(action)
``` 
The Ruleset dispatch method resolves the action based on the closure returned by start_action:
``` python
    result = rules.start_action(self._handle)
    if result: 
        state = json.loads(result[0])
        result_container = {'message': json.loads(result[1])}
        action_handle = result[2]
        action_binding = result[3]
        #other details removed for clarity
        for action_name, message in result_container['message'].iteritems():
            break
        #other details removed for clarity 
        self._actions[action_name].run(c, action_callback)
```
**About result[2] and result[3]**  
These are just handles used for correlating the state and connection to be used when completing or abandoning the action.  
**A note on result[0]**  
The first result from start_action is the rule context. You can write rules against the rule context and you can change the rule context during action execution. Starting an action means "leasing" a rule context. No other process will be able to see the rule context while it is leased to a given process. This allows for distributing action execution among compute hosts in a "competing consumers" fashion. Also please note you can bind the same ruleset to different redis servers. Rule context will be sharded among the redis servers based on 'sid'. This allows for datastore scale.  

Hope this helps,

Yeah, I see the issues with the external API. As far as I know, v1 is similar to v2 in terms of configuration (ip, oid, port, community, timeout). So to add v1 support, you could add a "version" argument, and have the public functions just call the proper version for v1 or v2.

The problem is, as you said, that doesn't work for v3 without contaminating the version-agnostic API calls with a bunch of v3-specific arguments.

The class approach would work well I think. It's a common idiom I've seen in other projects, and of course brings the benefits of using classes. 

Another option I've seen is having separate sub-modules for each version. An example of this would be:
```python
from puresnmp.v1 import get
get(ip, community, oid)

from puresnmp.v2 import get
get(ip, community, oid)
```
It's somewhat ugly, but it does have the advantage of providing the same API with only minor changes to the imports. Also, multiple versions could still be used in the same program, it would just require extra imports for each version:
```python
import puresnmp.v1 as v1
import puresnmp.v2 as v2
import puresnmp.v3 as v3
v3.get(ip, community, oid, user=username, key=key)  # Silly contrived example of v3
```

A little more work, but still infinitely better than PySNMP IMO.

Apologies, all.  I think I may have led us all on a wild goose chase here.

I think this all stems from my assumption that the `oX` tag at the end of the default filename that `gallery-dl` creates when downloading tumblr galleries was the same as the `oX` that appears at the end of the `name` parameter.  So in my efforts to introduce words to the filenames and not just IDs, my filename parameter was initially something like 
`{id}_{slug}_(name}.{extension}`
However, that means that the resulting files would come out reflecting the tumblr root filenames.  As a result of _that_, it meant that the resulting on-disk galleries would be in the wrong order as they are naturally sorted by the filename.
This also explains what I was seeing in the output logs with stuff coming out in _o2, _o1, _o3, _o4 order, as that reflects the tumblr `name` parameter.

I turned off all filename parameters, and just went with the default tonight to test it, and it actually comes out in the right order on-disk, even using a rearranged gallery as the source.  The default filename comes out with o1 o2 o3 in the order they appear in the gallery, even though the source filenames are  _o2, _o1, _o3.

When looking through `-K` to build the filename string I saw `offset` in that list - but there wasn't any indication that that meant "this is the index number of a picture in the gallery in a particular post".  
I looked at the tumblr API and saw 'offset' there as a parameter to a search/filter and thought it was something to do with that.

So I guess in summary:

- Tumblr images are named with an increasing numerical tag in upload order, `o1` `o2` `o3` etc
- Tumblr image galleries can be in an arbitrary order, completely independent of filename order
- The Tumblr API returns images in gallery order, not filename order
- gallery-dl default filenames are tagged with a label on the end o1 o2 o3 etc.  This number is completely independent of the o1 o2 o3 that may be on the tumblr filename, and is instead dependent on the order of the images returned by the API.  gallery-dl is actually working perfectly fine by default.
- It is very much not obvious what the `offset` keyword does when using -K, leading to the only obvious sources of numbering for someone constructing an altered filename the `name` or `filename` keywords.  `offset` sharing a name with a real tumblr API parameter doesn't help

So in reality, the only thing that needs fixing out of all this is making it clear what `offset` does to a casual user of the program.  Either rename it to something like `gallery_index` or decorate the output from -K to provide an explanation for it (and maybe other parameters).  Make it clear that if you want gallery order in your filenames that that's the parameter to include.

Okay, I see. These videos require a session cookie for the requests, but gallery-dl doesn't have it because OAuth doesn't deal with cookies..

Not sure if there is any elegant solution for this, I don't see it right now. The only workaround presumably is to add support for reading cookies in text form to gallery-dl from the command-line. This is not specific to Flickr, so this might be useful again in some other cases.
I think something along the line of how curl handles this, sending cookies with `-b "c=1; d=2"`, or better `-b FILE`, which is a "cookie jar" file, not sure if that is really standardized, but curl can create these as well. This happens bacause of [`os.path.join()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.join)'s behavior when using an empty string as the last argument:
```Python
>>> from os.path import join
>>> join("", "d1", "", "d2")
'd1/d2'
>>> join("", "d1", "", "d2", "")
'd1/d2/'
```

It adds a slash (or back-slash on Windows) to the end if the last argument is an empty string.

Your file has offsets of zero for each channel but has different absolute start times. The output from tdmsinfo looks like:
```
/
properties:
name: Test TDMS data
  /'Test Group'
    /'Test Group'/'Channel A'
      data type: tdsTypeDoubleFloat
      properties:
      wf_start_time: 2015-11-20 20:13:43.596313
      wf_start_offset: 0.0
      wf_increment: 1.0
      wf_samples: 10
    /'Test Group'/'Channel B'
      data type: tdsTypeDoubleFloat
      properties:
      wf_start_time: 2015-11-20 20:13:46.596614
      wf_start_offset: 0.0
      wf_increment: 1.0
      wf_samples: 10
```

So when you want to plot the data you could plot each channel against an absolute time or calculate the offset based on the absolute times. If you want to get a time track with absolute times for your channel you can set `absolute_time` to true in the `time_track` method, eg. `t = tdms_obj.time_track(absolute_time=True)`.

Thanks for the PR, I'd be happy to have a method that can export a Pandas DataFrame, but it looks like this is quite specific to your use-case, and might not work well for all users in general. This ignores the group name and just creates columns in the DataFrame based on the channel name, so won't work for files that have channels with the same name in different groups. The use of `dropna` might also be something that  not all users would like. And it would be nice if pandas wasn't required unless the `as_DataFrame` method was used.

I'd be happier with something more simple like:
``` python
def as_dataframe(self):
    """
    Converts the TDMS file to a DataFrame
    :return: The full TDMS file data.
    :rtype: Pandas DataFrame
    """
    import pandas as pd
    temp = {}
    for key, value in self.objects.items():
        temp[key] = value.data
    return pd.DataFrame.from_dict(temp)
```

Cheers,

@xoviat That's a good idea, I forgot it existed. But I think it's a bit limiting (no sided modifiers, keys must have vk) and we still need to solve the problem for other platforms.

I finished the code for modifiers + key, now available on the branch `suppress`. The contrived logic was isolated into a 24-state finite state machine (https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/suppress/keyboard/__init__.py#L129). I've been dogfooding it and it's been reliable.

An excellent side effect of this implementation is that it exposes the internal suppression engine, so I added high-level functions for [`block_key`](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/suppress/keyboard/__init__.py#L1058), [`remap_hotkey`](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/suppress/keyboard/__init__.py#L1023), [`hook_blocking`](https://github.com/boppreh/keyboard/blob/suppress/keyboard/__init__.py#L1065) and a few others. Key/hotkey remapping was a much needed feature that was almost impossible to do reliably before, and it's now much easier to add your own suppression logic.

The bad news is that there's zero support for suppressing multi-step hotkeys or hotkeys with multiple non-modifiers (e.g. `esc+a`). I'm still deciding on the relative worth of those features, suggestions welcome.

After this, the other two big projects are adding device ID detection to Windows, and a X backend. I think I'll focus on X support due to the requests for key suppression on Linux.

By traceback I found the reason of the error log.

It's because I called `consumer.close()` . The code of function `close` in [/kafka/conn.py](https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/0.9/kafka/conn.py):

```python
def close(self, error=None):
        """Close socket and fail all in-flight-requests.
        Arguments:
            error (Exception, optional): pending in-flight-requests
                will be failed with this exception.
                Default: kafka.common.ConnectionError.
        """
        if self._sock:
            self._sock.close()
            self._sock = None
        self.state = ConnectionStates.DISCONNECTED
        self._receiving = False
        self._next_payload_bytes = 0
        self._rbuffer.seek(0)
        self._rbuffer.truncate()
        if error is None:
            error = Errors.ConnectionError()
        while self.in_flight_requests:
            ifr = self.in_flight_requests.popleft()
            ifr.future.failure(error)
```

`consumer.close()` passes no error and so the code below is executed:

```
        error = Errors.ConnectionError()
        ...
        ifr.future.failure(error)
```

This may be misleading.

---

If i don't call `consumer.close()` explicitly, it may cause resource leak problems like `Too many open files ...` .

@nicolargo , there's nothing in the documentation to indicate that an AMP can be used to evaluate command output. I'm also not seeing a way to discard stderr.
This—
```
# upslist0 2> /dev/null ups.load
9
```
—becomes this—
```
Ups_load			1	Init SSL without certificate database
					9
```
—in the current best case, with the label malformed, useless stderr output included, and no obvious processing of the value (9%). I don't see a way to trigger a warning or error in Glances if the value is, for example, 75% or 90%. There's no intelligent formatting of the output, and no way to define it, either.

What I was really looking for was something like the output from lm_sensors that Glances already formats, displays attractively, and handles error reporting for.

Since it's impossible to write some custom script to be executed by Glances that can take into account the display width and height and perform formatting and line-wrapping, it's also impossible to avoid wasting a huge amount of vertical and horizontal space. This information—
```
# upslist0 |& egrep '(battery.(charge|runtime|temperature|voltage))|(input.(frequency|voltage))|(output.(frequency|voltage))|(ups.(load|status)):'
battery.charge: 100
battery.charge.low: 25
battery.charge.warning: 50
battery.runtime: 6365
battery.temperature: 18.9
battery.voltage: 55.7
battery.voltage.nominal: 48.0
input.frequency: 59.9
input.voltage: 121.9
input.voltage.nominal: 120
output.frequency: 59.9
output.frequency.nominal: 60
output.voltage: 119.8
output.voltage.nominal: 120
ups.load: 9
ups.status: OL
```
—could be displayed on as little as one line with some abbreviation and grouping. The only way to do that, however, would be if Glances actually parsed the output as variables, which it doesn't.

(I tried sending some tabs and ANSI escape codes for color highlighting through AMP. The escapes were printed as literal characters and the tabs ended up uneven somehow. So it looks like Glances is in complete control of the display, and I can't customize it even the least bit by writing a wrapper to attempt to handle some of the parsing and formatting myself.)

One item that occurred to me, involving saved space? Glances could use some global options to optionally hide or "collapse" output as long as it doesn't indicate a warning / error / critical state. Intelligent retrieval of NUT variables—again, completely possible through the Python bindings the NUT project provides—could transform a list of "healthy" UPS variables into a space-saving "UPS: **GOOD**", or even just a "UPS" section header in green, with no contents.

Likewise, it would be great to be able to hide filesystems that aren't over a certain percentage used, sensors or a laptop battery that are within normal parameters, and so forth. The same could be done with the process listing, by providing the ability to hide processes that aren't performing storage I/O or network I/O, aren't utilizing more than a configurable range (1-5%?) of CPU time, and so forth.

If this is something you'd consider, I'd be happy to split it into a separate issue.

I'm using pyminizip in a thread in a python gui application with a progress() function which updates a progress bar on the gui while it's compressing a large folder of text files.

But similarly, in a command line python script the progress function could be used like:

```
from __future__ import print_function
import os
import pyminizip

files = [f for f in os.listdir('.') if os.path.isfile(f)]

def progress(count):
    percent = 100.0 * count / len(files)
    prog = '#' * int(percent)
    prog += ' ' * (100 - len(prog))
    print ('\r[%s] %0.2f%%' % (prog, percent), end="")

pyminizip.compress_multiple(files, "archive.zip", "1233", 4, progress)
```
This displays a nice pretty progress bar on the command line while it's compressing all the files in the current folder.

Using VS 2008 to generate subprocess executable fixes the issue with AVs. Virus total scans for subprocess.exe created with Python 2.7 32-bit and 64-bit (Detection ratio 0/61 and 0/57):

https://virustotal.com/en/file/0e5301bc6b3cdba528541a2fd0a44fba89410b2cefc83683e343c14ed7d7429b/analysis/1492162062/

https://virustotal.com/en/file/d59fa52bba8ba0d419695c4630718ac8578fb6675487a53e87401a027ce45d72/analysis/1492162231/

So to resolve the issue the build_distrib.py tools should be updated to build subprocess executable using VS 2008. Currently it uses subprocess executable built by the last Python version used and it loops Pythons from lower to highest (2.7, 3.4, 3.5, 3.6).

> 遇到同檔名的可以跳出提示
> 檔名可以是相同的，但文件hash是唯一的。關於這個問題我個人對於comiccrawler的理想狀態是帶有檢驗機制的，遇到同檔名的就進行hash校驗，確定同一個文件（image_001.jpg）就跳過，同檔名但不同文件的就用image_001_1.jpg保存下來

要校驗 hash 的必須條件為先把檔案下載下來，和目前 Comic Crawler 的運作方法不同。目前的運作方法是這樣的︰

1. 抓取集數列表
2. 取得各集第一頁的 URL
3. 抓取第一頁
4. 取得圖片的 URL 和原始檔名
5. 產生檔案名（e.g. image_001.jpg 或原檔名 image_xxx_1.jpg）
6. 檢查檔案是否已存在資料夾內
	- 否 -> 抓取圖片，存至資料夾
7. 抓取下一頁，回到 (4)

如果檔案已存在於資料夾內，就不須下載圖片。

若要校驗 hash︰

1. 抓取集數列表
2. 取得各集第一頁的 URL
3. 抓取第一頁
4. 取得圖片的 URL 和原始檔名
6. 產生檔案名（e.g. image_001.jpg 或原檔名 image_xxx_1.jpg）
7. 檢查檔案是否已存在資料夾內
	- 否 -> 抓取圖片，存至資料夾
	- 是 -> 抓取圖片，檢查 hash 是否相同
		- 否 -> 產生新檔名並回到 (6)（省略抓取圖片）
8. 抓取下一頁，回到 (4)

無論檔案是否存在資料夾內，都必須執行下載圖片的動作。

Throughout the 7 year history of APScheduler, you are the first to ask for job history retention. There are many ways to do it and I'm not convinced at the moment that its inclusion in APScheduler itself is the right choice. I might want to use a time series database to record job submissions from all job stores. Then again, not everybody might want to store such history for reasons of disk space or just data retention policies.

Changes of this scale would have to wait until v4.0 anyway, and my plan was to tinker with a new design using my other project ([asphalt-tasks](https://github.com/asphalt-framework/asphalt-tasks)) as a staging ground. I have beginnings of an alternate system of job submission tracking there, though history still requires event listeners saving them to some database. That scheduler is better designed with better decoupling and perhaps more suitable as a testing ground but is still quite incomplete due to me being swamped with work and other F/OSS projects.

That because the EBU does a full normalization so if there are really low
lows and really high highs it takes those drops and pikes into account so
as not to cuss clipping. So the out come will be with in + or - 5dB. But
over all it will "sound" to be around -26 to the ear. Only case I've found
where that is a draw back is when you her movie's that have 2.0 sound that
are taken from 5.1 or 7.1 sound in those cases it will make the sound over
all lower because in most cases the LEF ie subwoofer Channel get warped
into the 2.0 sound and the channel is amped up so that your subwoofer will
play the bass at about the right levels when played with the other channels.

> I did some digging into your prior correspondence regarding the calls invoked by Elastic Beanstalk. I noticed in your Cloudtrail logs that there are DescribeAccountLimits calls happening with some frequency, made by the same 'limits' Role associated with the instance profile for EC2 instance INSTANCE_ID_REDACTED (where your awslimitschecker app is running if I understand correctly.) The event source for these calls is Cloudformation, and this API retrieves your account's CloudFormation limits, such as the maximum number of stacks that you can create in your account.[2] I know very little about the app, but it appears that a) it's attempting to retrieve limits for all the supported services [3] and b) the methodology it employs to do so will vary by service. In Cloudformation's case, there's a built-in API for this, but with respect to Beanstalk it appears that the app is attempting to check the 'elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-ACCOUNT_ID_REDACTED' bucket (the naming convention conforms to the default bucket created by Beanstalk) for saved configurations under the 'resources/templates' prefix.[4] 

> I conferred with one of our Elastic Beanstalk subject matter experts and was informed that there is a recently released Beanstalk API/command that is much better for checking EB-related limits, DescribeAccountAttributes.[5] It appears that the awslimitschecker app hasn't been updated to utilize this new API. 

> With all of that said, this particular AccessDenied error can be mitigated fairly easily. The 'limits' role doesn't have permission to list the contents of the 'elasticbeanstalk-us-east-1-ACCOUNT_ID_REDACTED' bucket. If you add 's3:ListBucket' to the role policy 'limits', it should resolve the issue. I can see that the policy is based on an example provided in the 'awslimitschecker' documentation, so you might consider bringing this up in their community channel [6] if you have the opportunity.

@svisser unicodecsv mimics Python 2's csv module in that regard. unicodecsv is, after all, "a drop-in replacement for Python 2's csv module which supports unicode strings without a hassle."

You can write a header row manually by using the "[the underlying `writer` instance](https://docs.python.org/2/library/csv.html#csv.DictWriter)" (Python 2.7 docs), which in unicodecsv's instance would be [the underlying `UnicodeWriter` instance](https://github.com/jdunck/python-unicodecsv/blob/master/unicodecsv/__init__.py#L149). To expand on the example from the ``csv.DictWriter`` docs using unicodecsv:

```python
import unicodecsv as csv

with open('names.csv', 'w') as csvfile:
    fieldnames = ['first_name', 'last_name']
    dwriter = csv.DictWriter(csvfile, fieldnames=fieldnames)

    # Look closely at the next line.
    # We use the DictWriter instance's underlying ``UnicodeWriter`` instance.
    dwriter.writer.writerow(['First Name', 'Last Name'])

    dwriter.writerow({'first_name': 'Baked', 'last_name': 'Beans'})
    dwriter.writerow({'first_name': 'Lovely', 'last_name': 'Spam'})
    dwriter.writerow({'first_name': 'Wonderful', 'last_name': 'Spam'})
```

Forgive me for a moment while I rubber-duck this... =)

For an AutoField or IntegerField upon which HashidAutoField and HashidField are based on, if you try to look up an ID with something that doesn't cast to an integer, like "asdf" instead of 100 or "100", they will throw a ValueError exception. This is where this behavior originally stems from (though I can't remember why exactly I made it a [TypeError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#TypeError) instead of [ValueError](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#ValueError)).

Normally this never happens with AutoFields and IntegerFields because people write their URL regexes to only match integers, such as `r'book/(?<pk>[0-9]+)/'`, so doing something like `mysite.com/book/asdf/` instead of `mysite/book/1/` will throw a 404 because Django can't find a valid URL to match "asdf" to. However, if you were to write your URL regexes to accept any character (`[0-9a-zA-Z]`), Django would throw a ValueError exception when the lookup failed. But when we talk about Hashids, it's just not possible to filter out invalid Hashids with a regex as they could possibly be any character, and it's still possible to have an invalid Hashids string made up of valid characters.

(On a side note, with the new URL dispatcher in Django 2.0, I'm thinking I could create a custom Path Converter to allow a path string like `path('book/<hashid:pk>')` that validates the characters and if it's a valid hashid based on the field it's for before it even calls your view. But alas, I'm supporting 1.8-1.11 right now, and even after 2.0 is released, I plan on supporting 1.11 until 2020, same as Django.)

Before version 1.3, the check was done in `get_prep_value()` for both lookups and when you save the instance, and it definitely needs to make sure you're saving either an integer or a valid Hashid string, and throw an exception if not. Just like you can't save, "asdf" to an IntegerField.

However, in the last version (1.3) I created a completely separate code-path for Lookups versus prepping the field to save to the database. I decided for 1.3 to keep the functionality the same (which was throw a TypeError), but I have been giving some thought to changing this now that I have the option. I believe it definitely makes sense to change this for non-exact lookups (iexact, contains, icontains) which would fix #16. However, I'm not exactly sure if I should change it for exact lookups as well.

One thing I've learned, is that the field is used in a lot of really complicated parts of Django that I didn't even know about in the beginning, and I'm afraid there can be some weird side-effects I'm just not even aware of or testing for.

So I have these options:

1. Leave as-is... `filter()`, `get()`, `get_object_or_404()` and other lookup methods will need to be wrapped in try/except blocks to catch the TypeError. This was the only option before 1.3.
2. Change non-exact lookups to an EmptyResultSet (which results in a DoesNotExist down the line), so a lookup like `Book.objects.filter(id__contains="asdf")` will return an empty queryset, but `Book.objects.filter(id="asdf")` will throw a TypeError.
3. Change all lookups to return EmptyResultSet when an invalid Hashids string is given.

And corollary to that, I could add an settings flag to enable throwing exceptions like previously just in case someone was really reliant on that functionality.

What I'm worried about is the weird cases I don't know about, and what this change could mean for them. I personally would like this, as in the project I originally developed the module for, and in the sandbox, I've overridden  `get_object()` to catch the TypeError, but it's messy and ugly, and I would prefer to not have to do it.

```
    def get_object(self, queryset=None):
        try:
            return super(AuthorDetailView, self).get_object(queryset)
        except (ValueError, TypeError):
            raise Http404("Not Found")
```

I don't know... thoughts? Anyone else have any input, especially with cases where they want a lookup to return TypeError instead of just an empty queryset result?

And would option 3 be a breaking change that should warrant a version 2.0? Or is it more of a bug-fix? Should I add an option to enable this EmptyResultSet functionality that is off by default in 1.4, then later after more feedback release 2.0 with the option on by default?

For PostgreSQL native versioning and alembic, I ended up doing this in Alembic upgrade:

```
def create_initial_import_command(class_name, transaction_id):
    target_table = Base._decl_class_registry[class_name + 'Version'].__table__
    source_name = target_table.name.replace('_version', '')

    t_cols = []
    s_cols = []
    for c in list(target_table.columns):
        cname = c.name
        if cname.endswith('_mod'):
            s_cols.append('true')
        elif cname == 'transaction_id':
            s_cols.append(str(transaction_id))
        elif cname == 'end_transaction_id':
            s_cols.append('null')
        elif cname == 'operation_type':
            s_cols.append('0')  # insert
        else:
            s_cols.append('"%s"' % cname)

        t_cols.append('"%s"' % cname)

    command = 'INSERT INTO "{target_table}" ({target_columns}) ' \
              'SELECT {source_columns} FROM "{source_table}"'

    return command.format(
        target_table=target_table.name,
        source_table=source_name,
        target_columns=', '.join(t_cols),
        source_columns=', '.join(s_cols)
    )

...
    op.execute('INSERT INTO transaction (id) values (DEFAULT)')
    op.execute(create_initial_import_command('Foo', 1))
    op.execute(create_initial_import_command('Bar', 1))
```

Where `'Foo'` and `'Bar'` are declarative class names. This will insert a new transaction with default id (which presumably gets the id 1 as the table was newly created along with its SERIAL). This works with PostgreSQL native versioning (with PropertyModTrackerPlugin), and possibly with some other configuration.

OK, I think I see the confusion now...  

One core means Spark will only assign one task at a time to that executor.  However, once the task is assigned to the executor, Spark can execute that task on a Python worker from a [pool of workers](https://github.com/apache/spark/blob/master/core/src/main/scala/org/apache/spark/api/python/PythonWorkerFactory.scala).

So, the "bad" reserve task is retried multiple times (on the executor), and one of those times, it will succeed if on a "fresh" worker from the pool.

@mdipierro Using whatever atomic version of `update_or_insert()` each database engine provides would be nice. But I think that adding an argument controlling the lock is not much of a solution. Anybody who knows what they're doing will set the lock on as soon as they find out that the parameter exists. Because the impact on performance will be negligible. The record gets locked either way and locking it a fraction of a millisecond earlier makes no difference. Everybody else will have to deal with random and completely unnecessary errors.

@niphlod The record will get locked by `update_record()` whether you like it or not.

@gi0baro Would you care to quote the specific part of PostgreSQL docs which says that rows modified by `UPDATE` will be unlocked early? Or you could just run this snippet of code to see for yourself that rows modified by `UPDATE` stay locked until the end of transaction:

    connstr = 'FIXME'
    def create_tables(db):
        db.define_table('lock_test', Field('foo', type="string"))
    print('Create tables')
    db1=DAL(connstr)
    create_tables(db1)
    db1.lock_test.insert(foo='foo')
    db1.commit()
    db2=DAL(connstr)
    create_tables(db2)
    db2.commit()

    try:
        print('Update 1')
        db1(db1.lock_test).update(foo='bar')
        print('Update 2')
        db2(db2.lock_test).update(foo='baz')
        print('Commits')
        db1.commit()
        db2.commit()
        print('Results:')
        print(db1(db1.lock_test).select(db1.lock_test.ALL))
    except:
        db1.rollback()
        db2.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        db1.lock_test.drop()
        db1.commit()

On the other hand, this snippet of code won't deadlock because `Table.__call__()` uses `limitby=(0,1)` to read (and lock) only the first record found.

    connstr = 'FIXME'
    def create_tables(db):
        db.define_table('lock_test', Field('foo', type="string"))
    print('Create tables')
    db1=DAL(connstr)
    create_tables(db1)
    first = db1.lock_test.insert(foo='foo')
    second = db1.lock_test.insert(foo='foo')
    db1.commit()
    db2=DAL(connstr)
    create_tables(db2)
    db2.commit()

    try:
        row = db1.lock_test(foo='foo', _for_update=True, _orderby=db1.lock_test.id)
        print(row)
        row2 = db2(db2.lock_test.id == second).select(db2.lock_test.ALL, for_update=True)
        print(row2)
        db1.commit()
        db2.commit()
    except:
        db1.rollback()
        db2.rollback()
        raise
    finally:
        db1.lock_test.drop()
        db1.commit()

> For instance, in your example if you change the first line with a FOR UPDATE, thread B cannot update the record until A execute `update_record`.

Correction: Thread B cannot update it until Thread A commits the transaction. And that's the whole damn point why the record needs to get locked in the first place.

@piiswrong The difference of this operator and numpy's shuffle is not the default axis. This performs a batch operation but numpy does not. For example, if 2D array is given, numpy shuffles the order of the rows (not individual elements), i.e., the performed elementwise permutation is exactly same for all columns. The operator in this PR would shuffle the elements in each column independently if it shuffled along the first axis. I'm not sure why numpy chose such a way. However, I think that the batch operation is more natural and consistent with the behavior of other operators, especially with `sort` which also reorders the elements. Shuffle along the last axis is also consistent with other operators. Moreover, `mx.random` package already has an operator (`multinormial`) which behaves differently from the operator with the same name in numpy. If we need to follow numpy despite of these, I'll change the behavior of the operator to comply numpy.

@indygreg once the binaries show up under https://ci.appveyor.com/project/wez/watchman/build/b1418/job/5uxrilwn2750ska7/artifacts you may want to try the same sort of experiment that we did here; I'd love your input.

The idea is that we're passing in a buffer size that is too big for the kernel to decide to cache when it makes its own kernel side memory allocation, and since the notifications are very busy, we end up not filling that buffer up very much at all with the effect of thrashing the kernel memory allocator layer in an expensive way.

In #549 I added a configuration option to control the size that we use for this buffer.  We did a quick run today with it set to 4096 and it seemed to help, but it wasn't done in the most scientific manner.

You can set this value by putting it in the `.watchmanconfig` file in the repo root:

```
{"win32_rdcw_buf_size": 4096}
```

or in the system global config file.  That is (hilariously|tragically) set to `/etc/watchman.json` in our windows build, so the latest iteration of the pull request is setting it to `C:/ProgramData/facebook/watchman.json` which is not the best location either, but at least slightly more deterministic.

I suspect that we should really do some experiments with differing sizes for this to see if there is a sweet spot.

Several people have raised the issue that logging backoffs as ERROR on exceptions is too aggressive. Of course, it's hard to say if other people are satisfied with this behavior. Nonetheless, I'm inclined to tweak this behavior because it's something that has come up repeatedly.

But rather than add some additional API surface that configures the level, I think we may be able to address this by making all backoff events log as INFO rather than error. That way, people who want the extra verbosity can the 'backoff' logger level to INFO and those who do not can leave it at its default ERROR level.  (We would still log as ERROR on giveup.)

We saw the same issue as @elipapa and had troubleshooting it took a while. I'll document the fix here, in case someone finds it with Google or something.

We're using CentOS 7. At some point the python-tornado package in CentOS 7 was updated from python-tornado-2.2.1-8.el7.noarch to python-tornado-4.2.1-1.el7.x86_64. It should be noted that the package changed architecture from noarch to x86_64 at the same time.

The packaging has a bug of some sort, which causes the system to have leftover tornado code in /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado even though the current code installed by the x86_64 package is in /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/tornado. Python seems to prefer the outdated noarch code to the current x86_64 code.

The fix is to remove the directory /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado and its contents manually.

@Tarrasch we have a --overwrite flag that we used to use to quickly redo a task if there was a bug in the task or while developing. It just makes complete return false. This has recently caused problems at Houzz when trying to redo a task that depends on other tasks of the same type, as it caused a big cascade of redos and actually prevented non-leaf tasks from running because they would always have incomplete requirements. It seems unsafe to ever not specify a parameter value in a task constructor under #1182. I expect this sort of behavior from global config classes, but not from object constructors. I guess we'll have to be more explicit in all of our requires functions if we want to get the desired behavior.

This sort of thing probably causes bigger problems at Houzz because most of our tasks are impala/hive queries which all share the same small set of classes and differ only in the query and a few other config options. It's a bit bizarre though. If I run a daily query with --overwrite, only the daily queries that it depends on will also become overwrite. Queries that are scheduled hourly and thus have a different class will not be re-run and neither will tables that are filled in from mapreduce jobs. You can imagine how confusing this is for the end-user who is not a programmer and only interacts with luigi by writing queries in a yaml config and running the command-line. We infer dependencies automatically from the queries, so having dependencies behave differently depending on their classes seems very random.

So currently the linters/formatters and any other providers are declared at the[ top of the PythonLanguageServer](https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server/blob/master/pyls/python_ls.py#L27) class. 

The idea behind this (although not implemented at the moment) is that the [initializationOptions](https://github.com/Microsoft/language-server-protocol/blob/master/protocol.md#initialize) can be used to choose between different providers. Notice we already use multiple linters at the same time and could easily configure different formatters on initialisation.

In terms of linter / formatter config, again some of this could be passed in the `initializationOptions` but we already [support reading config](https://github.com/palantir/python-language-server/blob/master/pyls/providers/lint.py#L62) from `setup.cfg`, `tox.ini` files etc. that are available in the workspace. Unfortunately this is currently done manually instead of relying on the tools' built-in config parsing meaning results may vary. An improvement here might be to shell out to pycodestyle / pyflakes / any other linter setting the working directory to the workspace root and then parsing stdout.

Upon further examination of the protocols, and a bit of background reading, I'm pretty sure that the Spotify Connect works something like this:

Firstly, in order to get Spotify Connect running, you need two key files: libspotify_embedded_shared.so, and libspotify.so. The former clocks in at a fairly small 170KB, whilst the latter clocks in at 1.3MB. Combined with what I could see from a quick glance at the decompiled versions of the two, libspotify_embedded leaves most of the heavy lifting to libspotify:

libspotify_embedded appears to run a simple HTTP service that is resolved by the Spotify client after being discovered via zeroconf. This library looks as though it manages the online status (availability) of the speaker to devices on the network, and handles communication with clients. 

The communication looks like it happens through an IRC-like protocol, called spirc, which is available here: https://github.com/neandrake/spirc. In addition, Spotify look to have implemented a wrapper for spirc, called GAIA, to use in their clients, though I would guess it doesn't modify the spirc protocol in any way...

Anyway, once a Connect Device is found and available, it looks as though spirc sets up a connection, through which the client sends commands and their respective parameters to the speaker, which libspotify_embedded i responsible for parsing and interpreting.

From here, libspotify_embedded takes the commands from the client, interprets them, and outputs them in the correct manner to the relevant function in libspotify, which is subsequently responsible for getting, playing and controlling the music speaker-side.

I am in the process of documenting each of the function calls as completely as possible from the reversed code, and will do my best to delve into the Connect HTTP API, once I'm near a connect speaker again. I suspect and hope that quite a bit of the code from the Local HTTP API has been reused, or at least the structure has been kept broadly the same. Hopefully if I'm broadly right about this, once I get access to a connect speaker, I can get a barebones setup running on nodejs ;)

In the meantime, if anyone wishes to examine the libraries, or help in any way with this, I have uploaded the unzipped firmware to Dropbox here: https://www.dropbox.com/sh/mjslxd2j1np9fwm/AACfNouVyPM9DZyXHPg3TA80a?dl=0 drop me a line if interested ;)

Credit to http://divideoverflow.com/ for the initial research into Connect which has been mentioned in here.

Hi @rromanchuk, do you still have the problem?

> I just noticed that it wasn't possible to use the script manually and that seems broken.

It's not broken because `monkeytype/cli.py` is not a script, it's a library module (which happens to contain a function that we use as the entry point for our setuptools-generated script).

Unfortunately Python kind of requires that you maintain a clean separation between scripts and library modules, or else `sys.path` (not to mention `__name__`) gets all kinds of confused.

So yeah, for clarity/ease in development, we should probably add an included script that isn't generated in your virtualenv bin/ dir by setuptools.

> the solution is to just pipe the output of --completion and put the output into your .profile

The solution is to pipe the output to `/usr/share/bash-completion/completions/pipenv` or `~/.local/share/bash/completion/completions/pipenv`, but surprisingly few people seem understand much at all about completion.

Of course, this does nothing to speed up the actual tab-completion part, despite the fact that most completion definitions (i.e. the non python-autogenerated ones) are written to avoid shelling out to any external commands more than necessary, using shell builtins wherever possible and generating then hardcoding (via shell arrays) anything application-specific in the 99% of cases where it won't change without source code changes.

I think our greatest aid is the keyword ago. No native speaker would write relative date without adding 'ago' considering the duplicity of meaning that would ensue otherwise. Hence, we probably need an approach where we can switch between relative and absolute dates context on the basis of the presence of keyword ago in the datestring for such cases. If ago is not present, the date should be considered as relative and only be translated for calendar literals (day/week/month/year) instead of month names.

Hi, this is indeed expected behavior; when you create a translation, Django stores a complete model with title + description field. The fallback is only used when there is no translation model stored for a specific language, and in your case, there is, even though it's an explicit blank field. From what I can tell, that could also be on purpose.

If you want an empty description to mean, take the contents from the fallback, that's fine. That could either be built into parler (using something like `TranslatedField(any_language=True, fallback_when_empty=True)`), or by adding some `get_description()` method to your model that makes two calls on it's own using `safe_translation_getter`

I have been reading up on the use/abuse of **kwargs and I understand your misgivings about implementing it.  Using the pymatgen.io.vasp.interfaces.py classes, we have a vasp_interface_defaults.yaml file similar to the InputSets YAML files.  This file allows us to easily customize various parameters like KPOINTS settings and Custodian Parameters without scripting in each change to every python script (see below for example).  We were using this to modify a couple different handler parameters (FrozenJobErrorHandler timeout and MaxForceErrorHandler threshold) for HSE and optical properties.  When running Optical calculations, there were long periods (especially with high KPOINT grids) where vasp.out would not be written to and the simulation would be terminated.  This was one of the biggest desires for implementing it into the Defaults File.  We wanted to keep using the FrozenJob handler to prevent wasting CPU hours, but needed to give VASP enough time to run the Optical Calculations.

But our function for loading handlers for Custodian was copied from fireworks_vasp.scripts and it was limited in how it fed handler parameters down to the handlers.  I'll see if I can work more logic into that function to eliminate the need for **kwargs in the handlers.

As discussed IRL with @jirikuncar:

1) Missing upgrades shouldn't happen (upgrades should only depend on upgrades in it's own module). Likely this is temporary problem, because of all the module externalisation happening these days. 
2) Missing upgrades usually means spelling mistake or similar in the depends on attribute, so normally we should bail out and not run the upgrades. 

Possible solutions:
1) Add an ``--ignore-missing`` option to run command
2) Add a ``mark as applied`` command + an apply command (i.e. run an upgrade plus anything it depends on).

So,
django.db.models.query.iterator now fetches an initial query via compiler.execute_sql(). Since iterator is not overridden in djangotoolbox, a select request now gets sent to the base compiler which has historically only expected count queries. Ideally, select logic should be in the base compiler's execute_sql() method, but this is not the case. In the new flow, that initial query sent to execute_sql() returns None and results_iter is called to handle the query as usual.

results_iter now has a results parameter which defaults to None. If it's None, the query is evaluated. In django 1.8's compiler code, that parameter is part of some kind of caching mechanism that I didn't look into.

Buildozer install by replacing the current APK (to prevent erasing invalid or not your own APK). The command (adb install -r (-r indicate remove)) is the one used by buildozer.

The error "INSTALL_FAILED_UPDATE_INCOMPATIBLE" is because the signature of your APK is not the same comparing to your previous installed APK. You can have this if you either do a debug APK, then try to install a "release" APK (with your own signature).

I would advise not to change buildozer behavior to prevent removing apk that you don't own.
If you use release/debug quite often, use buildozer's profile to generate 2 differents apk with different domain name.
If you don't, maybe it's your docker that generate each time a new debug key? or signing process goes wrong on your side?

Anyway, don't hesitate to post a solution for others that look at it.

This is how I use it now:
I added a construction to the place where the app is inited, so that it can return a flask app when needed, something like:
```
def run(actually_run=True):
    app = connexion.App(__name__, specification_dir='./swagger/')
    api = app.add_api(swagger_file)
    if actually_run:
        app.run(host=HOST, port=PORT, ...)
    else:
        return app.app
```

Then a live server can be started from tests that need it:
```
import requests
from flask_testing import LiveServerTestCase
from main import run_application

class MyTest(LiveServerTestCase):

    def create_app(self):
        app = run(False)
        return app

    def test_server_is_up_and_running(self):
        response = requests.get(f"{self.get_server_url()}/an_endpoint_for_your_api")
        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, 200)
```

And we do not get the data where we should because we reuse the `data` position in the return list of [`get_full_response`](https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/decorator.py#L39) to return [the immutable `flask.Response` object](https://github.com/zalando/connexion/blob/master/connexion/decorators/produces.py#L119-L121) the user created.

Because the order of evaluation of the decorators respective responses in case of user returning a `flask.Response` object is:
- security decorator: returns whatever use sends back
- parameters validation: returns whatever user sends back
- validations decorators (whatever is set): returns the `flask.Response` that user sent back
- produces decorator: (calls get_full_response method) and returns `flask.Response`, status from response object, headers from response object.
- response validation decorator: (calls get_full_response method) and returns `flask.Response`, status from response object, headers from response object. (BOOM! :bomb: Bug happens here!)

adding `PANTS_TEST_PYTEST_OPTIONS="-vvs"` to Jenkins reveals that watchman seems to be having issues on CI (from the `pantsd.log`):

```
I0511 01:22:37.731731 20464 pants_daemon.py:137] starting service <pants.pantsd.service.scheduler_service.SchedulerService object at 0x7f22d4034d90>
W0511 01:22:37.735089 20464 pants_daemon.py:34] Exception in thread Thread-4:
W0511 01:22:37.735213 20464 pants_daemon.py:34] Traceback (most recent call last):
W0511 01:22:37.735316 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 801, in __bootstrap_inner
W0511 01:22:37.735423 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]     self.run()
W0511 01:22:37.735517 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]   File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 754, in run
W0511 01:22:37.735665 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]     self.__target(*self.__args, **self.__kwargs)
W0511 01:22:37.735821 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]   File "/mnt/xvdb/jenkins/workspace/pantsbuild/pants/PR-3376@2/src/python/pants/pantsd/service/fs_event_service.py", line 112, in run
W0511 01:22:37.735928 20464 pants_daemon.py:34]     raise self.ServiceError('watchman is not running, bailing!')
W0511 01:22:37.736026 20464 pants_daemon.py:34] ServiceError: watchman is not running, bailing!
```
which incurs a pantsd teardown (by design) as a means of resetting daemon state.

my current theory is that in Linux CI, the initial start of pantsd races the subsequent pants run that uses said pantsd's pailgun as its nailgun client endpoint. this incurs a teardown of pantsd - and the yet-to-be-forked pailgun runner -  out from underneath the nailgun client, causing a deadlock.

if this is true, it seems there are 3 opportunities to make things more robust here:
  1) safeguard the nailgun client against such deadlocks. iirc, there's a known instance of deadlock potential around the use of `readline` with a prescient TODO that should get addressed.
  2) make failure to launch watchman a fatal event when the daemon options are enabled. it's a dependency and this would've failed earlier in CI had this been fatal, revealing the issue sooner.
  3) figure out a better way to synchronize the pants runs in the pantsd integration tests and validate state between invocations (or find a better way to test this in general).

my example 

**stdin-test.py**
```python
import sys
import json
import logging

print("STARTED")
while True:
    line = sys.stdin.readline()
    print("GOT DATA")
    print(line)
    print("Above is data")
    data = json.loads(line)
    print(data)
    data['success'] = True
    logging.info(json.dumps(data))
    break
print("Finished")
```

**Runner.py**
```python
import json
from docker.client import Client
from docker.utils import kwargs_from_env

data = {'msg': 'hello-world'}

cli = Client(**kwargs_from_env())
print(cli.version())

container = cli.create_container(image="example/stdin-test" ,stdin_open=True)
cli.start(container)
s = cli.attach_socket(container, params={'stdin': 1, 'stream': 1})
encoded = json.dumps(data).encode()
print(encoded)
sent = s.send(encoded)
print(sent)
s.close()
print(cli.logs(container))
```

Hi @jstoxrocky,
I was planning to suppress the logs using [pytest.mark.filterwarnings](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/warnings.html#pytest-mark-filterwarnings]), however I could not get it working. I was curious why It was not working. So I went ahead and read the source of `pytest` and `warninngs`. It turns out that `filters`  that are [added last](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/04e82934659487ecae76bf4a2db7f92c8dbe0d25/Lib/warnings.py#L178) get higher priority. 
So the filter `'@pytest.mark.filterwarnings(ignore::DeprecationWarning')` was always getting lower priority than the following code:
https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/6b783c5ed18ad47bb89c0ce2119037c981d2d27c/web3/utils/decorators.py#L53
Here's a hack I came up with to get it working:
```python
import functools
import warnings

import pytest

class Filter(list):
    def insert(self, index, p_object):
        self.append(p_object)


def filterwarnings(filters):
    def decorator(to_wrap):
        @functools.wraps(to_wrap)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            with warnings.catch_warnings(record=True) as logs:
                warning_filters = warnings.filters[:]
                warnings.filters = Filter()
                warnings._filters_mutated()
                for _filter in filters:
                    warnings._setoption(_filter)
                return_val = to_wrap(*args, **kwargs)
                # log all uncaught logs
                # for warning in logs:
                #     msg = warnings.formatwarning(
                #         warning.message, warning.category,
                #         warning.filename, warning.lineno, warning.line)
                #     item.warn(msg)  # item obj in a pytest hook
                warnings.filters = warning_filters
                warnings._filters_mutated()
                return return_val
        return wrapper
    return decorator
```
and then use it as `@filterwarnings(['ignore::DeprecationWarning'])`. However, this is a hack and it can change intended behaviour.
 I was planning to implement this as a [pytest hook](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/writing_plugins.html#writing-hook-functions), but I don't think that it's worth.

`with pytest.deprecated_call()` seems like a better option to me because once the deprecated code is removed it will  error  if tests are left unmodified. However, `pytest.mark.filterwarnings` can remain as a stale check.

The problem is that you have the NSG and storage account in different resource groups but are specifying both by name. Thus the CLI is assuming that both resources are in `test-group` which isn't the case. If you specify the full ARM ID of the resource group, your command will succeed.

I will keep this issue open though, because the error from the service should indicate that the storage account referenced does not exist.

This is good feedback on the `az context` commands, thanks.

There is a bit of ambiguity between `az context`, `az account` and `az cloud` commands right now. This is partly due to the `az account` commands being ported directly from xplat CLI and xplat did not have a comprehensive 'multi-cloud' solution.
We plan to have `az` support Azure Stack and multiple API versions in the future so we needed a more comprehensive solution than what existed in xplat so the context/account/cloud commands in `az` is what we have right now.

Please comment on https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/issues/1633 if you have any ideas on how you think this can be improved!

The following modules have invalid versions:

The root cause here is that `docker` daemon always runs as the `root` user. The validation using `docker ps` is correct since `docker login` also needs elevated rights (so as `docker pull`, `docker push`, etc.).

I will fix the CLI warning to suggest users add a `sudo` in such cases.

Hi @reeshabhraj1, here is an update on why we do not support CSE on the block level:

The algorithm that we are using to perform client-side encryption is AES256 in Cipher Block Chaining(CBC) mode, which was chosen for its security. In short, using CBC means that to encrypt a block of plaintext, it is mandatory to first encrypt all the previous blocks. Please refer to this link for more information on CBC: [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Block_cipher_mode_of_operation#Cipher_Block_Chaining_(CBC)). Thus, to make the implementation manageable, our CSE encrypts the entire blob. If blocks were encrypted individually, then decryption would be much harder: we'd have to keep track of where each individually encrypted block breaks off (with padding or not). So unfortunately, block-level CSE is not supported in any of the Storage SDKs.

If you absolutely need encryption, there are two options that I can think of:

1. stick with the SDK's CSE, and make download faster by increasing concurrency
2. implement your own custom encryption algorithm to encrypt at a block level
 

Please let me know if you have any other question.

@aaronmurraywork in version 12.0.0 of BIG-IP, the APIs for this changed to require a "type" of pool to query for. Unfortunately, this was a breaking change in the API.

To get the information you want, include the type of the pool in your API call.

For example,

```
AAAA pool
pool_obj = mgmt.tm.gtm.pools.aaaas.pool.load(partition='Common', name=gtm_pool['Pool_Name'])

A pool
pool_obj = mgmt.tm.gtm.pools.a_s.pool.load(partition='Common', name=gtm_pool['Pool_Name'])

MX pool
pool_obj = mgmt.tm.gtm.pools.mxs.pool.load(partition='Common', name=gtm_pool['Pool_Name'])
```

Note that statement "the 8 timestamps of NTFS" is not a technically correct. As you can make up from my previous comment 8 timestamps (in the MFT entry) is the minimum, but can easily be 12 or more. If you include the timestamps in the directory index $I30 a "file entry" would have at minimum 12 timestamps and with a short and long file name at minimum 20 timestamps. Also see: https://github.com/libyal/libfsntfs/blob/master/documentation/New%20Technologies%20File%20System%20(NTFS).asciidoc

The TSK API (an therefore pytsk) tries hard to have NTFS look like EXT, which makes it IMO not the best solution for obtaining file system specific information.

Thanks for reporting this issue, there were indeed a couple problems with FP UFs. These should now be fixed, but there is still a performance problem that I wasn't to fix yet, but that will happen soon.

@delcypher  Yes, a goal is not the same as an ordered set of SMT commands and I don't think that's what they should be. If we need that sort of granularity, I think we should add a new layer of abstraction in between; at the moment, the SMT commands are never kept anywhere, they are only applied to the cmd_context's state and then forgotten. 

As you say, that's probably overkill. Most applications that use SMT2 commands as their interface to Z3 have their own strategy for tracking and updating the state of the solver/goals (many including incrementality) and I expect they want to keep it that way. Of course, it's useful for people who want to implement their own incremental SMT solvers based on Z3, but those users/applications are fairly rare, and they will usually have to understand the relevant parts of the codebase anyways.

Tere are 2 Ideas behind Livetime:

2. Ram Usage, if the cache only grows, you can't use this in long running processes with many files.
With livetime we can cleanup the dict from time to time.

Back to this (sorry about the delay!). Please ignore the above nonsense :) (got things mixed up / wrong blocks). I initially meant that the block at 0x80 does not embed the literal pool that follows it (which is the case  whenever a block is followed by a literal pool), and in that sense, I considered it was truncated since I was expecting this literal pool to be lifted as well as part of the same block. That said, I guess this only makes sense if we make the (arbitrary) assumption that literal pools should be represented as part of the last block of each section (which is more or less where the compiler inserts them, although they are technically inserted after the last block). To clarify, this is not an issue per se, but more the question of how to represent literal pools at the basic block  level, and if it makes sense to do it at all (as opposed to leave it for "higher-level" analyses to figure it out). They're not code, but they are part of the text segment, and in some use cases (e.g., lightweight analyses modifying the text segment), it is useful to have them represented without having to analyze memory access patterns. Now, for analyses that actually "execute" the blocks, it doesn't matter, and the current behavior is fine.

Bottom line: if that sounds like something that could be useful to anyone else, is it better to 1) implement a (heuristic-based) way to represent literal pools at the pyvex block level, or 2) decide to leave it for higher-level analyses? (And perhaps add a CFG node property for this? But we might not always recover everything this way).

I don't think this is possible with nearest neighbor because the default behavior is to only get 1 nearest neighbor. Even if you got more than 1 neighbor I don't think you could be sure that X number of neighbors all fell within the target grid cell. For non-KDTree based resampling this is more likely since you normally perform some kind of weighted average so you would know exactly how many total input pixels when in to the target cell. However, this would still require keeping track of the individual counts which would require that the input being an integer, preferably no bigger than an 8-bit integer, or performing some kind of histogram calculation with buckets.

I think the easiest way to do this is as you've described with two remap calls. Or maybe given the newer `ll2cr` preparation step for `ewa` resampling this could be done as a separate method to the actual resampling.

The DDB field that holds the item version is a string. This is great, because it means you can chose whatever versioning scheme you want. So, if you want to use natural numbers, you can, but you can also have versions like `2016-01-01`. The downside to storing versions as strings is it means that the sort order goes to pot when you get past 9 versions of a key. The lexicographic sort order of 11 key versions might be:
```
foo - 1
foo - 10
foo - 11
foo - 2
foo - 3
 ... etc
```
So, we left-pad the version if you use `-a`. If you want to chose some other versioning scheme, you do that instead.

The above is helpful, but I still don't have concrete understanding. Could you confirm the followings about SR300? 
- Intrinsic parameters for both color camera and IR camera (=3D camera) are already calibrated by Intel when the SR300 is manufactured.
- It's not possible to change/modify these intrinsic parameters. (Or, is it possible to change by using SDK?)
- To calibrate the extrinsic parameters (R, T) for the color camera, we can use OpnCV's calibration method + checker board.
- Once after getting the extrinsic parameters of the color camera, we can calculate the extrinsic parameters for IR camera by using the transformation matrix between the color and the IR camera, which we can get by calling `get_extrinsics`.

I suspect this to be a bug in the calibration process. It seems like MM-Color and MM-Depth tables are empty. As a work-around, I believe you can get the relevant extrinsics by multiplying extrinsics from Depth to FE by extrinsics from FE to MM (this multiplication might cause some small degradation in precision, that's why librealsense tries to read from dedicated tables)

I was just writing my own version of a modelserializer ;)

It seems from my quick searching that the general way its being done at companies which are currently using graphql is to use 2 mutations - create and update

(However the version i was just working on i was adding a field called pk to the input and then checking if it existed. If you would include a pk then it would update otherwise it would create a new item (this is similar to the way django currently decides if it should run a INSERT or UPDATE in sql - [https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/instances/#how-django-knows-to-update-vs-insert](url) )

The problem with that approach is that the user may have already specified a value for pk to refer to something else (though i should think that if we are dealing with a `ModelSerializer` the user would not override the pk field) )

Even though this is the way django deals with the difference between update and create - i think we should follow consensus on the graphql side for frontend facing apis

Another issue i was dealing with was what if you want more than just the mutation fields back, for example, on creation of a new object, if you wanted the primary key of the new object, or one of many other possible fields, how would you be able to return that. As far as i understand the relay spec doesnt allow for that, but i was working on a method where you could instantiate a modelmutation with a return type which was a subclass of `DjangoObjectType`

> @wjakob

Yeah, I overlooked that objects can be sometimes be converted to `bool` even without the magic method.

> @aldanor 
> Do we want to have global implicit bool conversions? (which looks a bit scary to me as most everything can be converted to a bool)

I think that going completely broad should be fine (i.e. anything that isn't an error for `PyObject_IsTrue`):

1. Overload resolution will not be affected thanks to the new two-phase system.
2. Python happily coerces most things to `bool`:

```python
def foo(arg):
    if arg:
        print("yes")
```
```pycon
>>> foo(object())
yes
```

I think it's fine for pybind11 to have the same behavior, regardless of type or magic methods. Besides, it's simpler not to maintain a special pybind11-specific whitelist of what can be converted to `bool` when Python already has one in `PyObject_IsTrue`.

The motivation is to catch a fairly common case where someone accidentally tries to use a `std::shared_ptr<Derived>` with a `std::unique_ptr<Base>` (or vice versa)—we can't cast a derived holder to a base holder in such a case, which breaks our ability to handle inheritance.

I *think* this particular case is fine, though: we ought to fix the check to recognize `std::unique_ptr<T, D>` as a default holder (even if the `D` doesn't match between base and derived holders).  @trelau's workaround just basically makes both of them look like non-default holders, which ends up passing the check.  (You could still do problematic things, like mixing a unique ptr and shared ptr holders: we just won't catch them in such a case.)

By "on error 2/674", I meant that I suspect you're seeing the first cosmetic problems of what might be a much deeper incompatibility --- I meant that you've gotten past the first error, and there might now be 673 more to solve.

The `cymem` error you see there means that the header file that ships with `cymem` isn't being read accessed correctly. Thinc access `cymem` through its C API, so it needs the .pxd declarations. This might be not supported in PyPy, or it might just be a configuration issue.

I do care about job/env parity, even for a speed penalty, because I value the immediate, isolated feedback I get when something fails. (_Which Python version?_ _Which framework version?_)

Reduce `tox.ini` By A Section
-------------------
What if we agreed on a naming scheme and generated environment values automatically? Say, 

- `<framework><major><minor[minor]>` would inject a `FRAMEWORK=major.minorminor` env value in Travis (e.g. `py36-django111` => `DJANGO=1.11` on a Python 3.6 Travis job).
- A `[travis:env]` section would then be there to override those values. If the section is present at all, empty or not, the automatic scheme could be disabled.

This would reduce the `tox.ini` file by one section for the typical use case.

As the above is both very common and matches the scheme Tox already uses for Python (`py36` => `python3.6`) it should be alright for the Tox project.

Can Travis Sections Be Created Dynamically?
---------------
Can the `env:` and `matrix:` sections on Travis also be generated on the fly? Because I'm really worried about the messy `env:` and `matrix: exclude:` sections.

I agree that generating the `.travis.yaml` file physically would be only a compromise, to say at least.

Background:
```python
In [8]: range(0,100)[0:100:1]
Out[8]: range(0, 100)
``` 

If one  thinks in terms of generators then slicing is also not supported.

Lastly while slicing is pythonic, it has to do with existing sequences (that's also why by default one can't really slice a generator as mentioned above).

This syntax creates a sequence by using a slice, and hence the behavior of the syntax is not predictable, unless one has been involved in the discussion. Moreover  it is impossible to document.

I am deeply sorry for those who used this syntax.

Hey Luke,

Thanks for the update! One solution you should consider if it's possible to estimate the memory requirements of your tasks is using the user-defined resource labels (see https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/1236) to define a "memory" label. Instructions are in the PR. You can define a new label "MemoryBytes" which is the amount of bytes that the machine has (in ray start) and the amount of bytes required to run a task (in the decorator). The scheduler will make sure that the memory requirement of the currently scheduled tasks (as specified in the decorator) is smaller than the max defined in the node.

Let us know if that helps!

This however doesn't account for the amount of memory in the object store, so if that's the issue we should brainstorm some more about a good solution.

-- Philipp.

Whoa, whoops. And of course codecov chose this moment to flake out...

Did a little experiment:

```python
class CM:
    def __enter__(self):
        pass

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, exc_tb):
        # <insert code here>

with CM():
    raise KeyError
```

When `__exit__` is `return False`:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 9, in <module>
    raise KeyError
KeyError
```

When `__exit__` is `raise exc_value`:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 9, in <module>
    raise KeyError
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 6, in __exit__
    raise v
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 9, in <module>
    raise KeyError
KeyError
```

When `__exit__` is bare `raise`:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 9, in <module>
    raise KeyError
  File "/tmp/asdf.py", line 9, in <module>
    raise KeyError
KeyError
```

So `raise` saves a traceback frame compared to `raise exc_value`, but `return False` gives an even shorter traceback.

I suppose the next step in reducing traceback clutter will be to refactor `NurseryManager.__aexit__` friends so that it calls so that we use `raise` as little as possible... the obvious step would be to make `_nested_child_finished` return the collected exception instead of raising it.
